content: And once again there is a Cisco at the center of it all, with the picks and shovels for all and the expansive vision to go with it. Its name is Nvidia. Cisco was a real company with real revenue and real innovation. Yet its valuation soared far beyond sustainable levels. Nvidia is genuinely important. That does not mean its valuation is rational.
Sometimes, we see bubbles. Sometimes, there is something to do about it. Sometimes, the only winning move is not to play.

A glorious folly. The AI boom is a glorious folly — genuinely impressive technology wrapped in an unsustainable valuation story. The last time we saw this combination was the dot-com era. We know how that ended.

Companies buying advanced GPUs are depreciating them over six-year schedules. AI hardware becomes outdated too quickly to justify such long schedules. A more realistic useful life might be closer to three years. This practice inflates earnings for AI buyers, making returns appear stronger than they actually are. Expect significant write-downs between 2026 and 2028.

Many companies invest not only for returns but also out of fear of being left behind. This spending cycle may collapse once capital conditions tighten or performance expectations shift. FOMO-driven capex is not the same as economically justified capex. The difference matters enormously for terminal valuations.

Highlighted Bloomberg graphics showing circular partnerships between Nvidia and OpenAI — and between hyperscalers buying each other's compute. Revenue that flows in a circle is not revenue. It is a way of making balance sheets look busier than the underlying economics justify.

Nvidia's true owner earnings are lower than reported figures because stock-based employee compensation significantly dilutes shareholder value, even when buybacks partially offset it. Reported earnings and economic earnings are two different things. Wall Street reports the former.

Scion Asset Management disclosed over $1 billion in put options on Nvidia and Palantir in Q3 2025 SEC filings. $187.6 million in puts on Nvidia. $912 million in puts on Palantir. The fund also holds calls on Pfizer and Halliburton — companies in sectors with tangible physical constraints and undervalued assets.

Drew parallels to dotcom-era capital expenditure patterns. Current AI spending mirrors the fiber optic overbuild of 1999-2001 — an enormous investment in real infrastructure that nonetheless produced catastrophic losses for investors when the supply overwhelmed the demand that justified it.

Questions about undisclosed financing arrangements that may artificially inflate demand. If the seller is also the lender, reported demand is not independent demand. Nvidia denies such practices exist. But the pattern of hyperscalers buying compute and then selling AI services to fund the compute purchases deserves scrutiny.

The $912 million Palantir put position — the largest single bet in the AI short thesis — reflects a view that Palantir's valuation prices in a government AI monopoly that no company has ever been granted, over timelines no government procurement cycle has ever delivered, at margins no software company has ever sustained at scale.
People say I didn't warn last time. I did, but no one listened. So I warn this time. And still, no one listens.

The depreciation write-down cycle will hit between 2026 and 2028. Companies bought hardware at peak prices with artificially long depreciation assumptions. When those assumptions get corrected — either voluntarily or by auditors — the earnings impact will be large enough to change the narrative.

When news of Scion's Nvidia and Palantir put positions became public, both Nasdaq and S&P 500 fell sharply. Palantir dropped as much as 16% despite having just beaten revenue expectations. The market's reaction to Burry's bets — even before any fundamental change — demonstrates the fragility of sentiment-driven valuations.
